title: The Lender’s Cockpit
subtitle: Tools & risk heat maps

hook.metaphor: A pilot doesn't fly by gut feel — they fly by a cockpit full of instruments. Credit professionals are the same. Behind every lending decision sits a stack of tools: humble spreadsheets, serious code, big enterprise platforms, and one deceptively simple dashboard — the risk heat map — that lets a busy executive grasp the danger at a glance.
hook.scenario: You're handed a credit portfolio and asked, 'How risky is this, really?' You could send a 40-page spreadsheet. Or you could send one coloured grid that an exec understands in five seconds. Which lands?

learn: Start humble: **Excel is everywhere.** It's still the cornerstone for the vast majority of actuarial and risk teams — flexible, universal, and good enough for an enormous amount of analysis. Never be embarrassed by a spreadsheet.

When data gets big or models get serious, teams reach for **code** — usually **Python** or **R**. These handle millions of rows, build the predictive models from Level 4, and automate work that Excel can't.

For running credit at scale, there are **enterprise platforms**: **Moody's** (deep banking and credit-risk analytics), **Provenir** (automated, real-time credit decisions), and **LogicManager** (broader risk management for mid-market firms), among others. These are the industrial machinery behind big lenders.

And then there's the tool you'll actually see in meetings: the **risk heat map**. It's a grid — **likelihood** on one axis, **impact** on the other — coloured green (safe) through amber to red (danger). It turns 'risk = likelihood × consequence' (Level 1!) into a picture anyone can read. The whole point is *communication*: it lets you show executives the high-likelihood, high-impact threats without a single formula. You've been building one all along — that coloured cell on the Approval Dial is a heat-map cell.

A tip that translates everywhere: for *exploring* data without writing code, beginners increasingly use **point-and-click visualization** tools like **Power BI** or **Tableau** — drag fields, get charts. They've made data literacy possible for non-coders, which is exactly the spirit of this whole course.
bridge: So at work, you can pick the right instrument for the audience: a spreadsheet to work the numbers, code or a platform to scale them — and a **heat map** to communicate the answer to people who don't want the maths. Matching the tool to the job (and to who's listening) is half of being effective in risk.

interactions.title: Read a heat map like a pro
interactions.prompt: One more pass at the Approval Dial — this time, ignore the number and just read the heat-map grid. Move the sliders so the lit cell sits in the top-right (likely × severe = red), then bottom-left (rare × minor = green). That instant green/amber/red read is exactly what a heat map gives an executive.

interactions.title: Right tool for the job
interactions.prompt: Match each tool to what it's best known for.

pairs.right: The universal cornerstone for everyday risk analysis
Code for big data and building predictive models
Deep enterprise analytics for banking & credit risk
Automated, real-time credit decisioning at scale
Point-and-click data visualization, no coding needed
A coloured grid that shows risk to execs at a glance

interactions.title: What's it for?
interactions.prompt: Sort each tool by its main role in the cockpit.

items.label: A detailed pricing model in Excel
A risk heat map for the board pack
A Provenir workflow approving loans in real time
A Python script training a default model
A one-page Tableau dashboard for executives

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “Credit teams run on a cockpit of tools — Excel and code to crunch it, platforms to scale it, and a colour-coded heat map to make the risk obvious to everyone else.”

checkpoint.q: Despite all the fancy platforms, the everyday cornerstone tool for most risk teams is still…
checkpoint.explain: Excel remains the universal workhorse for the great majority of actuarial and risk analysis.

checkpoint.q: The main purpose of a risk heat map is to…
options: Communicate likelihood × impact to people at a glance
Write the code for credit models
checkpoint.explain: It's a communication tool: a coloured likelihood × impact grid that non-specialists grasp instantly.

checkpoint.q: Power BI and Tableau are popular with non-coders because they…
options: Are the only legal tools for credit risk
Let you explore and visualize data by point-and-click, no code
checkpoint.explain: They make data exploration possible without programming — point, click, and you get charts.
